---
title: "Wrinkled Jointtail Grass (Coelorachis rugosa)"
slug: "coelorachis-rugosa"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/181307717/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Wrinkled Jointtail Grass"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Liliopsida"
tax_order: "Poales"
tax_family: "Poaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Liliopsida", "Poales", "Poaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/181307717/medium.jpeg" alt="Wrinkled Jointtail Grass" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/lygodium63">
lygodium63</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107761789">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Liliopsida
-   **Order:** Poales
-   **Family:** Poaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=160776)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in wet pine flatwoods, savannas, and disturbed wet sites.

## Natural history notes

A perennial grass characterized by its upright stems and distinctive jointed seed heads with a somewhat wrinkled appearance. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as ditches, marsh edges, and wet fields. It grows during the warmer months and can form clumps in open, seasonally wet areas.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/160776-Coelorachis-rugosa)
-   [Local profile](https://regionalconservation.org/ircs/Database/plants/PlantPage.asp?TXCODE=Coelrugo)
